description: The supplied image is extremely low-resolution and presents as a small circular emblem composed of a blue peripheral ring, a dominant red rectangular central area, and a white lower section, bearing no visible axes, legends, scale bars, or alphanumeric labels. Consequently, no direct visual inference can be made regarding the Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel microstructures, tensile properties, or phase constituents discussed in the accompanying textual context. The figure does not exhibit recognizable SEM, EBSD, TEM, XRD, or stress-strain features, nor does it clearly depict a physical sample, device, or synthesis route. Because the pixel content is unreadable and no caption is provided, its specific scientific role—whether a schematic icon, a micrograph thumbnail, or a process marker—remains unverifiable. Any connection to the H70–H100 solution-treated specimens, their duplex ferrite-austenite microstructures, or TRIP-effect deformation mechanisms relies solely on external text rather than visual evidence.

This figure presents four scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs showing the microstructural evolution of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel after solution treatment at different temperatures. Subfigure (a) shows sample H70 (700 °C, 1 h), where nearly all original austenite decomposed into martensite (M) and spheroidal M5C2 carbide distributed along δ-ferrite (δ-F) grain boundaries and among martensite laths, with 12.2% retained austenite. Subfigure (b) shows sample H80 (800 °C, 1 h), where M5C2 carbide disappeared and martensite reverse-transformed to austenite (A), revealing δ-ferrite, austenite, and substantial strip-shaped α-ferrite (α-F). Subfigure (c) shows sample H90 (900 °C, 1 h), dominated by fine blocky α-ferrite, δ-ferrite, and increased austenite (33.8%). Subfigure (d) shows sample H100 (1000 °C, 1 h), where martensite reappeared alongside δ-ferrite, α-ferrite, and reduced retained austenite (12.9%). The series demonstrates that increasing solution temperature from 700 °C to 1000 °C drives carbide dissolution, ferrite morphology transition from strip to blocky, non-monotonic austenite volume fraction changes, and martensite reformation at high temperature, underpinning the alloy's phase stability and tensile behavior design.

Figure 2 presents the tensile mechanical response of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel after solution treatment at 700°C, 800°C, 900°C, and 1000°C (corresponding to specimens H70, H80, H90, and H100, respectively). Panel (a) displays true stress–strain curves with true stress in MPa versus true strain: no yield point phenomenon is visually apparent for any condition. As the solution temperature increases, the initial yield stress decreases progressively, while the 900°C curve shows a pronounced stress inflection at a true strain of ~0.03 and attains the highest true stress (~1120 MPa) and largest uniform elongation before fracture. Panel (b) plots the corresponding work-hardening rate (dσ/dε) against true strain, revealing distinct three-stage work-hardening behavior labeled S1, S2, and S3 across all temperatures. Stage S1 exhibits a sharp drop in work-hardening rate immediately after yielding. In stage S2, the 900°C curve uniquely shows a rising or plateau-like work-hardening rate between true strains of ~0.03 and 0.15, whereas the 700°C, 800°C, and 1000°C curves display only a gradual decline; this S2 behavior is attributed to a strain-induced martensitic transformation (TRIP effect). Stage S3 is characterized by a renewed rapid decrease in work-hardening rate, linked to deformation localization. The caption and supporting text infer that the absence of mechanical twinning and the pronounced S2 stage in the 900°C specimen indicate the TRIP effect as the dominant mechanism responsible for the superior strength–ductility combination.

Figure 3 presents four combined EBSD band contrast and phase identification maps (a–d) of solution-treated H90 Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel under tensile true strains of 0.03, 0.10, 0.20, and 0.27. In these maps, austenite is colored green, ferrite is gray, and martensite exhibits poor band contrast in black. Subfigures (a)–(d) collectively reveal the progressive deformation-induced martensitic transformation (TRIP effect). At 0.03 strain (a), both blocky austenite at triple-junction boundaries (red squares) and lath-like austenite between ferrite laths or inside ferrite grains (red ellipses) are visible. As strain increases to 0.10 (b) and 0.20 (c), blocky austenite gradually disappears while lath-like austenite persists. By 0.27 (d), nearly all retained austenite is lath-like, accompanied by abundant small-angle grain boundaries (green lines) reaching 51%. The calculated austenite volume fractions decrease from 31.4% to 4.7% across the sequence, confirming continuous TRIP-driven martensite formation during deformation.

Figure 4 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) band-contrast maps overlaid with Schmid-factor distributions for retained austenite (face-centered-cubic, colored via rainbow IPF lookup where red denotes high and blue denotes low Schmid factor) in Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel at true strains of 0.03 (a), 0.10 (b), 0.20 (c), and 0.27 (d), each accompanied by a Schmid-factor histogram; panel (e) summarizes the evolution of these distributions as 3D line plots of number fraction versus Schmid factor. At ε = 0.03, over 70 % of retained austenite grains exhibit high Schmid factors of 0.45–0.48. As strain increases to 0.10 and 0.20, the distribution peak shifts downward to 0.39–0.42 and then to below 0.34, indicating progressive consumption or rotation of high-Schmid-factor grains. By ε = 0.27, the volume fraction of austenite with Schmid factors < 0.34 drops to about 5 %. This demonstrates that retained austenite with lower Schmid factors tends to remain untransformed during tensile deformation, supporting the conclusion that Schmid factor governs the relative ease of slip and the mechanical stability of austenite in this TRIP steel.

Figure 5 presents the post-tensile-deformation microstructural characterization of H90 steel (Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel solution-treated at 900 °C for 1 h). Panel (a) is an SEM fractograph acquired at 2000× magnification (scale bar 50 μm) revealing a quasi-cleavage fracture surface featuring herringbone cracks, river patterns, secondary cracks, and localized dimples. Panels (b) and (c) are TEM micrographs showing high-density dislocation morphologies in δ-ferrite (scale bar 200 nm) and in martensite alongside retained austenite (scale bar 50 nm), respectively. The images collectively demonstrate that during tensile deformation, dislocations multiplied in martensite while wavy glide occurred in ferrite, leading to complicated dislocation structures that restrict dynamic recovery and enhance strength. These observations support the deformation mechanism involving dislocation slip, martensite formation as barriers to dislocation glide, and the resulting high work-hardening capacity in this duplex steel.

Figure 6 presents a schematic illustration of the martensitic transformation mechanism in the experimental Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel during tensile deformation. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure before deformation, where blocky austenite grains (white, labeled A) are located at ferrite (gray, labeled F) triple junctions, and lath-like austenite grains (red, labeled A) are situated between ferrite laths or embedded within single ferrite grains. Subfigure (b) depicts the microstructural evolution during tensile straining (indicated by vertical arrows): the blocky austenite transforms into martensite (blue, labeled M), while the lath-like austenite undergoes rotation prior to transformation. The caption and citation text state that blocky austenite with a higher Schmid factor experiences a triaxial stress state at phase boundaries and transforms first due to higher localized stress, whereas lath-like austenite with a lower Schmid factor rotates initially during deformation. This figure supports the core conclusion that austenite morphology and spatial distribution govern the TRIP effect sequence and deformation stability in duplex ferrite-austenite microstructures.

Figure 7 presents X-ray diffraction analysis and austenite fraction quantification for Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steels solution-treated at different temperatures. Subfigure (a) displays XRD profiles of specimens treated at 700 °C, 800 °C, and 900 °C before and after tensile deformation (post-deformation labeled with -F). Prior to deformation, diffraction peaks correspond to austenite (γ) including (111), (200), (220), and (311), ferrite/martensite (α) including (110), (200), and (211), and M5C2 carbide. After deformation, γ reflections weaken while α reflections strengthen, evidencing strain-induced martensitic transformation. Subfigure (b) plots austenite volume fractions before and after deformation for H70, H80, and H90 alongside martensitic transformation ratios. Initial austenite fractions increase from 12.2% (H70) to 20.7% (H80) and 33.8% (H90), decreasing after deformation to 4.5%, 5.8%, and 3.4%, respectively. Corresponding martensitic transformation ratios are 63.1%, 71.9%, and 89.9%. This confirms that higher solution temperatures raise austenite content but lower its mechanical stability, thereby intensifying the TRIP effect.
